round0.name: novelty_gated_contribution
round0.rationale: Assigned angle: novelty (sparse prior art) is the PRIMARY determinant, not a minor additive tweak. Rather than adding (1-anchor_mean_overlap) as one more summand alongside contribution depth (which only reaches train ρ≈0.65 and lets a well-typed but derivative paper's contrib score dominate regardless of how crowded its prior-art neighborhood is), I make novelty a multiplicative GATE on the whole contribution signal: score = gate(novelty) × contribution_depth − report-tells. gate ranges from 0.4 (dense prior art, mean_overlap→1, near-duplicate predecessor exists) to 1.0 (sparse prior art, mean_overlap→0, first-in-field). This directly encodes the causal story in FEATURES.md: 'dense, high-overlap neighborhood = incremental; sparse, low-overlap = genuinely novel' — a paper's stated depth of contribution only counts at full value if there ISN'T a crowded predecessor set already covering it; if prior art is dense, even a well-typed contribution gets discounted toward 40% because the paper's own novelty claims are less credible against real precedent. This is the fix for known failure mode (2): a deep-but-derivative diagnostic-accuracy or incremental-ML paper that scores high on contrib_wmean/contrib_peak now gets pulled down by the gate because its prior-art neighborhood is typically dense (high anchor_mean_overlap), whereas a genuinely sparse-precedent paper keeps its full contribution score. I kept contrib_wmean and contrib_peak (the two least-gameable, highest-single-feature-ρ contribution terms) as the thing being gated, since novelty alone (ρ 0.36) or contribution alone (ρ 0.51/0.38) are each weaker than the interaction, and additive novelty+contribution underperforms the gate (0.65 vs 0.68 in my sweeps). I kept the two established report-tell penalties (delta_max, n_deltas) at their known-good baseline weights (0.15/0.04) rather than re-tuning them per prior guidance that they are confound proxies, not causal signal, so I did not amplify them further even though small increases nudged train rho higher in a grid search — that would be overfitting the 40-point train split. I explicitly avoided anchor (rewards resolution success, a known bug) and anchor_max_overlap (redundant with mean_overlap, marginal gain, adds a 6th term for ~0.01-0.02 train rho — not worth the interpretability cost) and uptake_per_year/cd_index (dead on this corpus).

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.primary (normalized, all papers)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A single controlled-vocabulary field (e.g. primary_research | surveillance_report | review | trial_readout | method_paper) computed consistently for every paper, not just some.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The corpus confound this metric works around (report/surveillance papers piling up deltas) is really a genre effect, not a delta-count effect. Right now genre.yaml exists but is inconsistently schematised (some papers have genre.primary+novelty_prior, most only paper_type), so it cannot be used as a clean conditioning variable. A normalized genre field would let a metric condition contribution/delta interpretation on genre directly (e.g. only penalize dense deltas within report-like genres) instead of using delta_max/n_deltas as a blunt proxy that also misfires on genuine discoveries that happen to report big verified numbers (e.g. aki26, expert 70, delta_max 1.0).

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: downstream_assessment (follow-on, per-citer stance)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: For each in-corpus or near-corpus citer, whether it treats the cited paper as confirmed/extended vs. contested/superseded/failed-to-replicate.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: There is currently no feature that reads how the field actually reacted to a paper's novelty claim -- only raw citation counts (dead on this recent corpus) and the compiler's own untested reading of the paper's stated contribution. A stance-tagged follow-on edge would let the novelty-gate formula eventually swap out anchor_mean_overlap's backward-looking proxy for a genuine downstream-validated breakthrough signal once the corpus ages.

round0.name: depth_gated_novelty
round0.rationale: Core is importance-weighted contribution depth: contrib_peak (does it have ≥1 strong contribution) and contrib_wmean (mean novelty weight, breadth-resistant) at equal weight — this alone caps at ~0.55 train ρ, confirming depth is necessary but not sufficient. The genuine lift comes from a CAUSAL novelty term, not a sign-flipped proxy: (1 − anchor_max_overlap) recovers "distance from the single closest prior predecessor" (tested stronger than mean_overlap: 0.627 alone vs 0.357 alone on train). Critically, I gate this novelty term by `anchor` (the resolver's own confidence signal: 1.0 = real resolved neighborhood, 0.2 = compiler-estimated, and — the key fix — implicitly ~0 credit when anchor_max_overlap is None/unresolved, since an ungated (1−overlap) term wrongly gave a paper with an UNRESOLVED anchor (gie25, expert=7.7) a novelty score of 1.0, the same as a genuinely sparse-prior-art paper). This directly closes the known "anchor rewards resolution success, not density" bug by turning that same bug into a confidence multiplier instead of a standalone additive term — multiplying it by (1−overlap) makes low-confidence resolutions contribute little novelty credit rather than none/full at random. This single change moved train ρ from 0.645 (ungated novelty + depth only) to consistently ~0.73-0.74 across a broad weight plateau (not knife-edge). I kept only ONE minimal penalty term, n_deltas at a small coefficient (0.04), rather than the baseline's two (delta_max + n_deltas): n_deltas alone recovers most of the anti-report-genre signal that delta_max adds (0.685 vs 0.625 alone), so stacking both is redundant, and per the causal-vs-proxy framing n_deltas/delta_max are corpus-confound proxies (report genre correlates with delta-count in THIS corpus, not causally with breakthrough-ness) so I minimize reliance on it rather than eliminating it outright — a small penalty still meaningfully improves rank agreement (0.673→0.741 when added) and Occam's razor says a single small proxy term is more honest than tuning two. Verified aki26 (expert 70, delta_max 1.0 — the baseline's known misfire case) scores well here (0.99) because low anchor_max_overlap (0.35) with high resolution confidence (anchor=1.0) correctly earns strong novelty credit that outweighs its modest n_deltas=3 penalty.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.primary (normalized enum across ALL papers, not just some)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, consistently-schematized paper_type/genre classification (e.g. primary_research vs surveillance_report vs review vs guideline) present on every paper, not just the subset that currently has genre.primary + novelty_prior.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The delta features (n_deltas, delta_max) are currently the only way to infer 'this is a report/reestimate genre,' but they are a confound proxy that also fires on genuinely deep discovery papers that happen to report many verified numbers (aki26). A direct, complete genre field would let a metric condition novelty/depth credit on genre without laundering it through delta counts, and would cleanly separate 'is a report' from 'reports many numbers.'

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: anchor_resolution_confidence as an explicit standalone [0,1] field (distinct from the current overloaded `anchor` which conflates resolution success with the density-reward bug)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean confidence/completeness score for how well the prior-art neighborhood was resolved (real 2nd-resolver pass vs compiler-estimate vs unresolved), independent of any overlap magnitude.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: I had to repurpose `anchor` (documented as measuring resolution success, i.e. a known-buggy field never intended for this) as a multiplicative confidence gate on the novelty term, purely because it was the only proxy available for 'do we trust this overlap number.' A purpose-built confidence field would make the novelty-gating principled instead of a workaround, and would let the metric distinguish 'unresolved anchor' from 'resolved anchor with genuinely low overlap' — currently both collapse to anchor_max_overlap=None -> 0 via the None-guard, which is exactly the trap this formula had to route around.

round0.name: report-suppressor-interaction
round0.rationale:
Assigned angle was "aggressively subtract report tells from a depth core." I tried that literally first — depth core (contrib_wmean + contrib_peak) minus heavily-weighted raw delta_max and n_deltas (including normalized/saturating forms of n_deltas, e.g. n_deltas/10, n_deltas/(n_deltas+3)) — and every aggressive variant scored WORSE than the mild baseline (0.63-0.71 vs baseline's 0.712), confirming the brief's warning: n_deltas/delta_max are confound proxies (surveillance reports happen to pile up deltas), not causal drivers of low breakthrough-ness, so cranking their weight up just punishes any paper that reports numbers, including real discoveries (aki26: expert 70, delta_max 1.0, n_deltas 3). Simply adding a separate (1-anchor_mean_overlap) novelty term alongside raw delta subtraction only matched baseline (~0.716) — it corrects the WRONG conflated variable.

The move that actually worked, and is still a genuine 'report suppressor,' was to stop penalizing delta_max unconditionally and instead penalize the INTERACTION delta_max * anchor_mean_overlap. Rationale: a large quantified delta is only a report-genre tell when it's ALSO sitting in a dense, high-overlap prior-art neighborhood (i.e., the paper is re-announcing/updating an already-well-covered number — the actual signature of surveillance/burden/trial-readout reports). When delta_max is large but anchor_mean_overlap is low (aki26: overlap 0.21; jes26/zim25 donanemab trials: overlap 0.40), the same delta doesn't get punished, because a big quantified claim next to sparse prior art is a genuine novel result, not a report tell. Checking the corpus: the six papers with delta_max=1.0 split cleanly into report-genre (20225/20226/cum26, overlap 0.35-0.60, expert 4-6) vs genuine findings (aki26 overlap 0.21 expert 68; donanemab trials overlap 0.40 expert 22-39) — the interaction term separates them where raw delta_max cannot. This out-performed the naive aggressive-subtraction baseline (0.723 vs 0.712) and is stable across a coefficient sweep (0.30-0.45 weight on the interaction term all land in 0.70-0.72, i.e. not a knife-edge fit). Kept n_deltas at a small linear penalty (-0.04, same as prior baseline) since a light raw-count term still adds a touch of signal without dominating.

Net finding for the tournament: the "report-suppressor" angle is real, but only works when the suppression term is conditioned on the causal novelty signal (overlap) rather than applied to the raw report-tell feature alone — an unconditional aggressive subtraction of delta_max/n_deltas is a worse formula than the current baseline, not a better one.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized to a required genre.primary enum (e.g. discovery | method | trial_readout | survey_epi_burden | review) with consistent schema across all papers, not just some
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, non-gameable read of paper genre instead of inferring 'report-ness' through proxy interactions of delta_max/anchor_mean_overlap
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The delta_max*anchor_mean_overlap interaction is a good causal proxy but it's still indirect — it worked because in this corpus report-genre correlates with (high delta, high overlap), but that correlation could break on a different corpus. A clean genre field would let the metric condition on genre directly (e.g. down-weight surveillance/burden reports as a class) rather than reverse-engineering genre from two unrelated features, which is more auditable and more likely to generalize to corpora where genre and delta/overlap don't co-vary the same way.

round0.name: multiplicative-depth-x-novelty-max-overlap
round0.rationale: Assigned angle: multiply a DEPTH term by a NOVELTY term so a paper must be both to score high. Depth = a blend of contrib_wmean (confidence-weighted mean novelty-weight across contributions — depth that can't be padded by adding shallow items, ρ+0.50) and contrib_peak (does it have >=1 strong contribution, ρ+0.38), weighted 2:1 toward wmean since wmean alone drives most of the depth signal and peak adds a small boost for papers with one standout claim. Novelty = (1 - anchor_max_overlap): anchor_max_overlap is the closest single prior-work's overlap in [0,1] — this is the TRUE INVERSE CONSTRUCT for novelty per FEATURES.md (dense/close prior art = incremental; no near-duplicate predecessor = genuinely novel), not a sign-flipped confound proxy. I explicitly tried anchor_mean_overlap first (matches the guidance's suggested lever) which got only train ρ~0.52; anchor_max_overlap ("is there a near-duplicate predecessor at all") turned out to be the sharper gate and pushed ρ to 0.734 — a single close precedent should tank novelty even if the mean across a long, mostly-distant neighbor list looks fine. This is still principled: it is the same PRIOR-ART-resolved overlap construct, just the max instead of the mean, and it is monotonically related to the stated construct ("dense, high-overlap neighborhood = incremental"). No delta/n_deltas/genre-proxy terms are used — those are confound proxies per the brief (they penalize legitimate quantified reporting, e.g. misfire on aki26) and were deliberately excluded even though they'd nudge train rho a bit further. Guard default for anchor_max_overlap is 0.5 (neutral/average overlap), not 0, because defaulting missing prior-art resolution to 0 would falsely reward unresolved data as "maximally novel" (verified empirically: switching the guard to 0 dropped train ρ from 0.73 to 0.69). The multiplicative structure directly repairs failure mode (2) from the baseline: deep-but-derivative papers (diagnostic-accuracy studies, incremental ML methods) that the old additive/contribution-typing metric over-scored now get crushed by the near-duplicate-predecessor gate even though their contribution typing still reads as "deep."

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized to a single required genre.primary enum (e.g. primary_research | review | surveillance_report | trial_readout | methods_paper) on every paper, not just some
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, directly-usable genre signal instead of inferring 'is this a report/survey' indirectly through delta counts, which the brief flags as a confound-laundering trap.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Several high-n_deltas papers are legitimate discoveries (e.g. aki26, expert 70) that happen to report many verified numbers; without a clean genre field there is no causally sound way to down-weight surveillance/burden reports specifically without also penalizing quantitatively rigorous discovery papers. Right now genre.primary + novelty_prior exists for only a few papers, so it cannot be used reliably.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: downstream_assessment.yaml derived from later citing papers' stated relationship to this paper (replicates / extends / refutes / contradicts, with a strength score)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A true FOLLOW-ON signal of how the field actually reacted to a paper's claims (replication vs refutation vs silent superseding), rather than raw citation counts (uptake_per_year, cd_index) which are dead on this recent corpus and don't capture directional field consensus anyway.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: FEATURES.md explicitly notes there is no feature that reads the follow-on literature's assessment of a paper, only citation counts. This is the single biggest structural gap for a metric like ours: contribution novelty is currently only the compiler's own reading of the paper's text, never checked against how later work actually treated the claim, so a paper's self-declared 'new_paradigm' contribution can't be verified or falsified by what happened after publication.

round0.name: peak_dominant_novelty
round0.rationale:
Peak-driven design: contrib_peak carries strictly more weight (0.55) than contrib_wmean (0.45), so a single landmark contribution dominates the score — but I found pure contrib_peak alone (no wmean) tops out around train ρ 0.43-0.59 even with novelty added; contrib_wmean (a depth signal, confidence-weighted MEAN across contributions — not gameable by padding since it's per FEATURES.md explicitly NOT breadth) contributes real independent signal, so I kept it as a minority term rather than going pure-peak, honoring "breadth ignored" by never touching n_contribs (the actual breadth/count feature).

The novelty term is (1 - anchor_max_overlap): the closest single prior-art neighbor's overlap, inverted — a TRUE INVERSE CONSTRUCT (novelty *is* distance from the nearest predecessor), not a sign-flipped confound. I chose anchor_max_overlap over anchor_mean_overlap because in this corpus the max-overlap version measures "is there a near-duplicate before this" more sharply than an overlap averaged across a mixed neighbor set, and it measurably outperformed the mean variant (0.647 vs 0.568 train ρ with the same peak/wmean base) before the guard fix, and the gap persisted after.

Critical fix discovered mid-design: 2 papers (e.g. gie25) have anchor_max_overlap = None because the anchor never resolved (an unresolved-anchor case FEATURES.md explicitly warns about), and `(anchor_max_overlap or 0)` silently treats "unresolved" as "zero overlap = maximally novel" — this single-handedly promoted a genuinely weak survey (expert score 7.7) to rank #3 on my earlier draft. I switched the guard default from `or 0` to `or 1` (verified 0 papers in the corpus have a genuine overlap of exactly 0.0, so this only affects the 2 truly-unresolved cases, forcing them to a neutral/no-bonus novelty score instead of a false-maximal one) — this alone lifted train ρ from 0.568 to 0.702.

Deliberately excluded per the causal-vs-confound guidance: delta_max/n_deltas (report-tell confound proxies that would misfire on genuine quantified findings like aki26), n_contribs (raw-count breadth, explicitly out of scope for the peak-driven angle), and n_puffery (measured zero incremental effect on this train split — dropped to keep the formula minimal per the "prefer fewer terms" instruction).

Residual failure modes NOT fixed by this formula, inherited from contribution-typing itself: afi25 (vision/CNN survey, expert 10) and che26 (diagnostic-accuracy study, expert 23.3) still score mid-high because the compiler's contribution typing credits their methodological depth even though the panel judges them as derivative/incremental genre — this is the exact "over-scores deep-but-derivative papers" failure mode named in the brief, and it can't be resolved with contrib_peak/contrib_wmean/anchor_overlap alone since these papers don't have unusually dense prior-art overlap (0.28-0.48, not near 1.0) — they are penalized by genre, not by resolvable novelty distance.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized field: genre.primary enum {original_research, review_survey, diagnostic_accuracy_study, surveillance_report, clinical_trial_readout, methods_paper} applied consistently to every paper (not just some)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, paper-level genre classification independent of contribution typing or delta counts.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The two biggest residual misses in this formula (afi25 vision/CNN survey scored 0.706 vs expert 10; che26 diagnostic-accuracy study scored 0.701 vs expert 23.3) are genre effects: the panel discounts survey/benchmarking-style papers regardless of how deep their internal contribution typing reads, but contrib_peak/contrib_wmean cannot see genre, only claimed novelty depth. Currently genre.yaml exists but is inconsistently schematized (some papers have genre.primary + novelty_prior, most only have paper_type), so it cannot be used as a clean multiplicative gate without risking systematic bias toward the subset of papers that happen to have it populated. A uniformly populated genre field would let a peak-driven formula condition contrib_peak's credit on genre (e.g., discount survey/diagnostic-accuracy genres by a fixed factor) instead of relying on delta-count proxies that misfire on genuine discoveries.

round0.name: anti_padding_depth
round0.rationale:
Assigned angle: reward depth (contrib_wmean), punish breadth-padding (n_contribs) and unrealized over-claiming (n_puffery), and avoid the delta/genre confound entirely (no delta terms).

What I found: contrib_wmean is already structurally anti-padding — FEATURES.md defines it as a confidence-weighted MEAN over contributions, so stuffing a contributions list with shallow incremental_improvement/replication items (weight 0.15/0.1) drags the mean down rather than helping. contrib_peak ("does it have >=1 strong contribution") turned out to carry more per-feature signal than wmean alone on this train split (0.546 for 0.6*wmean+0.6*peak with no penalty at all), so I kept both rather than dropping peak to stay "pure" to the angle — the instructions permit using extra features when they carry signal, and peak is not a padding-driven quantity.

I then grid-searched only the two anti-padding penalty terms holding wmean/peak fixed near their natural range. Explicit punishment of n_contribs is a double-edged sword: raw n_contribs has ρ +0.35 with experts on its own (breadth of stated contributions weakly predicts breakthrough-ness in this corpus, likely because genuinely major papers do report more distinct findings). A strong penalty (-0.05 to -0.08) collapsed rho from 0.55 to 0.28-0.44 — punishing breadth as if it were pure padding actively hurts, confirming that raw n_contribs is NOT a pure confound the way n_deltas is. Only a small corrective nudge (-0.01) helped, nudging 0.546->0.569: it downweights the marginal case of two papers with equal peak+wmean where one padded a couple extra weak items. n_puffery is exactly 0 for every paper in this 40-paper train split (verified directly against corpus_scored.json), so its -0.15 weight is a zero-cost insurance term on train — it cannot move train_rho at all right now, but it is a causally sound term (a contribution voided by the anti-puffery lock is direct evidence of over-claiming, not a genre proxy) that should earn its keep on a held-out corpus that actually contains puffed papers.

I also tried adding a (1 - anchor_mean_overlap) novelty term per the known failure-mode note; it consistently reduced train_rho here (0.569 -> ~0.53-0.56 across weights 0.1-0.3), so I left it out — it's a real signal in principle but not orthogonal to what contrib_peak/wmean already capture in this corpus, and I did not want to pad my own formula with a term that doesn't earn its weight, in keeping with the "fewer terms, no gratuitous overfitting" instruction.

Net effect vs the un-penalized 0.6*wmean+0.6*peak base (0.546): my anti-padding penalties buy +0.023 rho on train. This is below the delta-based baseline's 0.71 (on this same train split with the same eval script) — but that gap is explained by the delta terms riding a report/survey-genre confound the task explicitly flagged as illegitimate (n_deltas ρ -0.32 is a genre tell, not a causal breakthrough signal), so I did not chase it. This formula is offered as the clean, generalizable anti-padding member of the ensemble: reward depth, mildly discourage inflating the contribution count, and hold a zero-cost trigger against detected over-claiming.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: contributions.yaml: padding_flag (bool) or contrib_dispersion (stdev of weight*confidence across contributions)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: Explicit statistical signature of 'many shallow contributions bolted onto one real one' vs 'several genuinely strong, independent contributions' -- i.e. distinguish high-n_contribs-with-high-variance (padding) from high-n_contribs-with-uniformly-high-weight (genuinely multi-faceted breakthrough).
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Right now n_contribs and contrib_wmean can't tell these two cases apart on their own: a paper with 6 contributions averaging 0.5 could be '1 strong new_method + 5 padding items' or '6 solid synthesis-tier findings.' A dispersion/variance field would let an anti-padding metric penalize the former specifically instead of penalizing raw breadth (which the data shows is weakly informative, not a pure confound).

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml: normalized genre.primary enum (e.g. primary_research / method / survey_report / epidemiological_burden_report / clinical_trial_readout) consistently populated for every paper, not just some
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, clean genre classification instead of inferring 'this is a surveillance report' indirectly through delta counts.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The task notes genre.yaml exists but is inconsistently schematised (some papers have genre.primary + novelty_prior, most only paper_type). A clean genre field would let a metric legitimately down-weight or separately calibrate report/burden-study genres without laundering that judgment through delta_max/n_deltas, which the causal-signal analysis flagged as a spurious corpus confound that misfires on genuine discoveries that happen to carry big verified deltas (e.g. aki26, expert 70).

round0.name: novelty_depth_interaction
round0.formula: 0.8*(r['contrib_wmean'] or 0) + 0.6*(r['contrib_peak'] or 0) + 0.3*(1-(r['anchor_mean_overlap'] or 0))*(r['contrib_wmean'] or 0) + 0.4*(1-(r['anchor_mean_overlap'] or 0))*(r['contrib_peak'] or 0) + 0.03*(r['frac_quant_verified'] or 0)
round0.rationale:
Structure: base depth (contrib_wmean + contrib_peak, the two features with the strongest per-feature signal, +0.50/+0.38) PLUS a novelty-gated depth bonus — (1-anchor_mean_overlap)*contrib_wmean and (1-anchor_mean_overlap)*contrib_peak — plus a tiny verified-delta bonus on frac_quant_verified. The interaction term is the point of the design: a paper's depth score should be worth MORE when the prior-art neighborhood is sparse (genuinely novel) and worth less when it's dense (derivative-but-deep). This directly targets known failure mode #2 — deep-but-derivative papers (sim26 multimodal-GNN, contrib_wmean=0.76 but anchor_mean_overlap=0.59; che26 diagnostic-accuracy study, wmean=0.51/overlap=0.48) get their depth term shrunk by the low novelty-gate, while genuinely sparse-prior-art discoveries (aki26 overlap=0.21, han26 overlap=0.37) get amplified. I kept both raw depth AND the interaction (rather than pure multiplication, which dropped rho to ~0.49) because pure multiplication zeroes out real breakthroughs whose anchor happens to be poorly/densely resolved for non-causal reasons (e.g. a well-covered subfield); the additive-plus-bonus form only discounts, never destroys, deep contributions.

The frac_quant_verified term is present per the assigned angle but genuinely weak here (ρ≈-0.13 alone, and a sweep showed adding it monotonically hurts train_rho past w≈0.03) — I capped it at 0.03, small enough to be a rounding-error-scale "tie-breaker credit for confirmed real numbers" without meaningfully punishing papers that report fewer verified deltas. I deliberately did NOT include delta_max/n_deltas as negative terms even though doing so pushes train_rho to ~0.68 (I verified this) — the tournament brief itself flags these as confound proxies (report-genre tells, not breakthrough-inverse signals) that misfire on genuine discoveries like aki26 (delta_max=1.0, expert=70) and would not generalize to a held-out corpus where breakthroughs also carry big verified numbers. I chose an honest, lower, causally-motivated train_rho over gaming this corpus's genre confound.

Ceiling exploration: I grid-swept ~600 weight combinations over {contrib_wmean, contrib_peak, novelty×wmean, novelty×peak, frac_quant_verified} and did not find anything materially above 0.58 within this feature family — this appears to be close to the ceiling reachable from novelty×depth interaction alone on this corpus, given the two dominant remaining error sources: (a) afi25 (a vision-transformer methods paper, expert=10) gets a spuriously LOW anchor_mean_overlap (0.29) — likely because OpenAlex prior-art resolution is thinner for niche ML-architecture combinations than for biomedical findings, so "sparse neighborhood" reads as novel when it's really a resolver-coverage artifact; (b) sal26 (a biomarker paper, expert=60.7) has dense overlap (0.54) AND low compiler-assessed depth (wmean=0.26) yet experts rated it a real find — dense prior art here means "extends a known biomarker family" which experts still valued, contradicting the anchor_mean_overlap-as-novelty assumption for incremental-but-important biomarker extensions.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.primary (normalized enum: original_research | surveillance_report | review_survey | clinical_trial_readout | biomarker_validation), applied consistently across ALL papers
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, directly-usable genre signal instead of inferring 'is this a report' through delta-count proxies.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The corpus confound this whole tournament angle is fighting (surveillance/burden reports piling up quantified deltas) is a genre effect, not a depth or novelty effect. Right now genre.yaml exists but is inconsistently schematised (some papers have genre.primary + novelty_prior, most only paper_type), so it cannot be used as a formula feature. A normalized field would let a future metric condition depth/novelty scoring on genre directly (e.g. only apply the delta-based skepticism to surveillance_report genre) instead of laundering it through delta_max/n_deltas penalties that also punish legitimate discovery papers with many verified numbers.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: anchor_resolution_confidence or anchor_coverage_domain (flag for which fields OpenAlex prior-art search has thin vs dense coverage, e.g. CS/ML-architecture vs biomedical)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: Whether a low anchor_mean_overlap reflects genuine sparse prior art or just thin resolver coverage for that subfield.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: afi25 (a vision-transformer paper, expert score 10, i.e. not a breakthrough) gets anchor_mean_overlap=0.29, which my novelty term reads as 'sparse prior art = novel' and over-credits it (candidate score 0.947, rank #11 of 40 despite true rank near the bottom). Without knowing whether the resolver's OpenAlex search actually covers ML-architecture literature as densely as it covers AD biomarker literature, low overlap is ambiguous between 'genuinely novel' and 'resolver didn't find the neighbors.' A confidence/coverage flag would let the formula discount the novelty bonus when coverage is known-thin.

round0.name: min_gate_depth_novelty
round0.rationale:
Implements the assigned min-gate angle literally: score = min(depth, novelty), so a paper cannot win by being deep-but-derivative (high contrib_wmean, dense prior art) or novel-but-shallow (sparse prior art, weak contribution). Depth = contrib_wmean (confidence-weighted mean novelty-taxonomy weight — chosen over contrib_peak/contribution because it cannot be inflated by padding with one strong + many weak contributions, and it beat contrib_peak and the composite `contribution` in the gate: 0.535 vs 0.447 vs 0.526 train rho). Novelty = (1 - anchor_mean_overlap), the causal inverse-construct the brief flagged as newly usable now that anchor resolution is complete for the corpus — low mean overlap with the prior-art neighborhood is literally sparse precedent, i.e. genuine novelty, not a sign-flipped confound. Deliberately excludes delta/n_deltas per the brief's confound-proxy warning: those correlate negatively only because report-genre papers happen to pile up deltas in this corpus, and subtracting them would misfire on genuine discoveries that also report big verified numbers (the brief's own aki26 counterexample).

Pure min(wmean, 1-overlap) alone scored 0.535 train rho but produces heavy ties at the novelty floor (many papers share anchor_mean_overlap≈0.65 → novelty floor 0.35), collapsing distinct depths into one bucket. Added `+ 0.3*contrib_wmean` as a same-feature tiebreaker: it never lets depth override the gate (the min() term still dominates whenever novelty is the binding constraint) but separates gated papers by how much depth they clear the floor with. This is a strict extension of the min-gate idea, not a different mechanism — same 2 features, one extra weighted copy of the depth term for intra-tier ordering. Weight swept 0.1-0.5; 0.3 was the stable optimum (0.554-0.559 across 0.28-0.32).

Known remaining failure mode (documented, not hidden): cum26 (a drug-development-pipeline report, expert score 6.3) still ranks #7 at 0.740 because its contributions are LLM-typed as high-novelty findings AND its prior-art neighborhood happens to be sparse — both depth and novelty proxies are fooled simultaneously by report genre, which the min-gate cannot catch since it isn't a genre signal. Fixing this needs the genre-typing extension noted below, not another delta-based patch.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.primary (normalized, mandatory on all papers, not just some)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, consistently-schematised classification of paper genre (original research / surveillance-report / pipeline-review / diagnostic-accuracy-study / etc.), populated for every paper the same way genre.yaml already does for a minority.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Both contribution-typing and anchor-overlap can be simultaneously fooled by report/pipeline-review genre papers (e.g. cum26): the compiler types their claims as novel findings, and OpenAlex prior-art search sometimes returns few close neighbors for aggregation-style reports. A direct genre gate (down-weight or exclude surveillance/pipeline-report genre) would fix this false positive without laundering it through delta-count proxies, which the brief explicitly warns against.

round0.name: ratio_depth_over_report_tells
round0.rationale:
Assigned angle: turn the delta penalty from a crude linear subtraction into a ratio that normalizes depth by report-tell density. The baseline (0.6*wmean + 0.6*peak - 0.15*delta_max - 0.04*n_deltas) subtracts a fixed amount per delta regardless of how deep the paper's contribution is — so a shallow report and a genuinely deep paper that happens to report several verified comparisons lose the same absolute penalty. Dividing instead — depth/(1+k*n_deltas) — makes the penalty *proportional*: a paper with strong depth (high contrib_wmean) survives a few report-tell deltas far better than a shallow one does, and the penalty saturates rather than accumulating linearly, which is the correct shape for "how much of this paper's signal is genre-report versus genuine depth" rather than "how many numbers did it announce."

Design choices, tested against train:
- Used n_deltas (COUNT) as the denominator's report-tell signal, not delta_max (MAGNITUDE) — n_deltas is the stronger tell (ρ -0.32 vs -0.21) and, critically, keeping delta_max out of the formula entirely fixes the baseline's known misfire on aki26 (expert 70, delta_max 1.0): aki26 has only 3 deltas, so it barely gets touched by the ratio, whereas the baseline's -0.15*delta_max term punishes it directly for reporting one big verified number that is part of a genuine discovery, not report-genre padding.
- Kept contrib_wmean (depth, not breadth — cannot be inflated by padding with shallow contributions) as the numerator being normalized, and added contrib_peak ("has ≥1 strong contribution?") as a separate additive term outside the ratio, since peak measures a different thing (best single claim) than the density-normalized-depth ratio measures.
- Grid-searched the ratio's damping coefficient (k in 1+k*n_deltas) and the two term weights on train only; k=0.6, weights 0.7/0.5 was the local optimum (0.667). Nearby settings (k=0.4-0.7, weights within +-0.1) all land in the 0.65-0.67 band, so the result is not a knife-edge overfit to one coefficient.
- Tried adding the causal novelty term (1-anchor_mean_overlap) on top of the ratio; it consistently *lowered* train rho by 0.01-0.03 in this construction (likely redundant with/noisier than what contrib_wmean+peak already capture on this corpus), so I left it out to keep the formula to 2 clean additive terms rather than pad in a feature that measured worse here — even though anchor-based novelty is a legitimate causal signal in principle, it did not earn its place in THIS formula.
- Did not use delta_max, n_puffery, anchor, uptake_per_year, or cd_index — the first two didn't help beyond n_deltas, and the last two are dead/noisy on this recent corpus per the brief.

This formula scores lower on train (0.667) than the linear baseline in this doc's earlier framing (0.63 reported, but I measured the exact baseline string at 0.712 on --split train, so the baseline is likely still stronger on raw train rho than my ratio version). The ratio construction is offered specifically because it targets the FAILURE MODE the brief called out (aki26 misfire, generalization risk of subtracting a proxy that happens to correlate with genre in this corpus) rather than to win train rho by brute force — it trades a bit of train-set fit for a more defensible causal shape (density-normalized penalty, not linear-count penalty) that should transfer better to a corpus where genuine breakthroughs also carry several verified deltas.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized field: genre.primary in a closed taxonomy (e.g. original_research / surveillance_report / clinical_trial_readout / review / methods_paper), consistently emitted for every paper
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: The actual report/genre confound directly, instead of inferring it through delta counts.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The brief notes genre.yaml already exists but is inconsistently schematised (some papers have genre.primary+novelty_prior, most only paper_type). If this were normalized, a formula could condition contrib_wmean and the delta features on genre directly (e.g. only apply a report-tell damping term when genre.primary == surveillance_report), instead of using n_deltas as an indirect, corpus-specific proxy for genre that risks penalizing genuinely deep empirical papers with many verified comparisons on other corpora.

round0.name: anchor-neighborhood-confidence-gated-novelty
round0.rationale:
Two terms. (1) 0.3*contrib_wmean — confidence-weighted depth of the paper's own strongest claims (ρ +0.50 alone, resistant to padding). (2) 0.8*(1-anchor_max_overlap)*min(anchor_n,9)/9 — a novelty term gated by resolution confidence. I explored the assigned angle directly: raw anchor_max_overlap alone already gives ρ -0.627 on train (1-overlap gives +0.627), far stronger than the -0.19/-0.20 reported for anchor/anchor_mean_overlap in the brief — the single closest prior-work neighbor is a much cleaner novelty signal than the mean over the whole resolved neighborhood, because reports/burden papers (bottom of the expert ranking: liu25h, ant26, 20226, wan25c, ahm26b) have a near-duplicate predecessor at overlap 0.83-0.93, while the top papers (ols25, han26, aki26) top out at 0.35-0.55.

I explicitly tested the assigned "few, low-overlap neighbors = first-in-field" hypothesis for anchor_n and it does NOT hold as stated: raw anchor_n has ρ +0.45 with expert (more resolved neighbors → higher expert score, not fewer), because in this corpus the reports/epidemiology papers that score lowest also have the *fewest* resolved neighbors (sor25 n=3, ant26 n=3, liu25h n=4) — likely a resolution-coverage artifact (FEATURES.md's own caveat: "very few + low overlap can indicate first-in-field, or just an unresolved anchor — be careful"), not genuine first-in-field sparsity. So I did not encode "fewer neighbors = better." Instead I use anchor_n as a *confidence gate* on the overlap signal: min(anchor_n,9)/9 saturates at 1.0 once the resolver has found a reasonably sized neighborhood (~9), and discounts the novelty credit when very few neighbors were resolved — i.e. "trust the low-overlap reading only when there was actually a neighborhood to compare against." This raised train ρ from 0.627 (overlap alone) to 0.753 (gated), and is causally defensible as a *reliability* weight on the true novelty construct, not a proxy substitution — unlike the delta-count confound flagged in the brief.

I deliberately dropped delta/delta_max/n_deltas entirely (confound proxies per the brief — they misfire on aki26 which has expert 70 and delta_max 1.0) and dropped anchor (the composite/resolution-success field, ρ +0.25 raw but known to reward resolution success rather than density) and contrib_peak (added ≤0.005 ρ for a 3rd term, not worth it). uptake_per_year and cd_index confirmed dead as documented, not used.

Caveat: 2/66 papers (1/40 train) have anchor_max_overlap=None (unresolved anchor, not verified zero-overlap); the required `(r['x'] or 0)` guard treats that as overlap=0 -> maximal novelty credit, producing one clear false positive (gie25-geographical-inequalities, expert 7.7, ranked 9th by this formula) whose anchor_n=4 wasn't small enough to fully suppress it via the gate. This is a substrate gap, not fixable within the allowed pure-arithmetic operator set (would need a conditional to distinguish "missing" from "zero"), so I flag it below rather than force a workaround.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: sota_anchor.yaml: anchor_resolved (bool) or anchor_confidence (0-1 float, distinct from the existing 'anchor' resolution-success score)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: Whether anchor_max_overlap/anchor_mean_overlap are real measured values vs. structurally absent because the resolver failed to build a neighborhood at all.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Under the mandated `(r['x'] or 0)` None-guard, a missing overlap is indistinguishable from a verified overlap of 0.0 (perfect novelty) using pure arithmetic alone -- a conditional/ternary is needed to tell them apart and none is permitted. This caused one confirmed false positive (gie25, expert 7.7) in the train set and affects 2/66 papers corpus-wide; on a larger or noisier corpus this could compound. An explicit confidence field would let a formula multiply the novelty term by real resolution confidence instead of silently defaulting missing data to the most novelty-favorable value.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml: normalized genre.primary (enum: primary_research / surveillance_report / epidemiology_update / clinical_trial_readout / review) present on every paper, not just some
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, first-class signal for 'is this a report/burden-update genre' instead of inferring it indirectly from delta-count proxies.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The brief flags that n_deltas/delta_max correlate negatively with expert score only because report-genre papers happen to pile up quantified deltas in this corpus -- subtracting delta is exploiting a spurious confound that misfires on genuine discoveries with large legitimate deltas (aki26, expert 70, delta_max 1.0). If genre were a clean, complete field, a metric could condition directly on genre (e.g. dampen surveillance/burden reports) without ever touching delta features, which would generalize far better to corpora where breakthroughs also carry large quantified deltas.

round0.name: contribution_quality_control
round0.rationale:
Control arm: use ONLY the [PAPER→LLM] contribution-typing signal — nothing from delta_ledger (report-tell proxy) or sota_anchor (novelty proxy) at all. Purpose is to isolate how far "does the compiler read this paper as claiming something novel" alone can carry the ranking, before any correction for genre confounds or prior-art density is added.

contribution (ρ=+0.51 alone) is itself already a peak+wmean composite (0.6·peak+0.4·wmean); adding contrib_wmean again re-weights toward "depth of the median claim, not just the single best one" — it downweights papers that have one strong contribution buried among many shallow/padded ones, and rewards papers whose claims are consistently strong. I scanned the achievable plateau (contribution:contrib_wmean weight ratios from 1.2:0.2 to 0.3:0.7, plus product and max variants, plus an n_puffery penalty) — everything clusters tightly at 0.53-0.55 train rho with no clear winner, so I kept the simplest, most interpretable combination (mirrors the composite's own internal 0.6/0.4 split) rather than hand-tuning decimals to chase a fourth-decimal gain.

Ceiling analysis: this control tops out around rho=0.55 on train — meaningfully below the 0.63 baseline that adds -0.15*delta_max-0.04*n_deltas. That gap is itself the finding: contribution-typing alone cannot distinguish "genuinely novel finding" from "deep, well-typed but derivative/incremental paper" (e.g., diagnostic-accuracy or ML-method papers score high on contrib_wmean because the compiler credits methodological depth, even when the underlying prior-art neighborhood is dense). Without a novelty-distance term (anchor_mean_overlap) or genre detection, contribution-quality alone is necessary but not sufficient — it measures "how good the paper says its own contribution is," not "how much white space it's occupying." I deliberately did NOT add delta or anchor terms, per the assigned angle, even though they're known to help, so this result should read as a floor/baseline for what a purely self-reported-quality signal achieves versus the fuller metrics other designers are building.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized to a single required `genre.primary` enum (e.g. discovery / method / report_survey / clinical_trial_readout / diagnostic_eval) across ALL papers, not just the ones that currently have it
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, directly-usable genre label instead of inferring 'is this a surveillance/burden report' indirectly through delta counts.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The corpus confound this control arm exposes is that contribution-typing alone can't tell apart a genuinely novel discovery from a deep-but-derivative diagnostic/ML paper, and separately the baseline metric launders a genre signal through n_deltas/delta_max (a proxy that misfires on quantified discovery papers like aki26). A first-class genre field would let a metric condition contribution credit on genre directly and legitimately, without the proxy's false positives/negatives.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: contribution_novelty_confidence or a resolver cross-check that ties each contribution's compiler_assessed_type against the sota_anchor neighborhood overlap for the specific claim (not just paper-level anchor_mean_overlap)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: Per-contribution grounding of the self-reported novelty type against actual prior-art distance, rather than a single paper-wide overlap number.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: contribution/contrib_wmean are pure self-report (LLM reading the paper's own framing); this control arm shows self-report alone plateaus at rho~0.55. A contribution-level (not paper-level) check against prior art would let a metric discount a claimed new_method/new_finding that turns out to sit in a dense neighborhood, which is exactly the failure mode of over-scoring incremental-but-well-argued papers.

round0.name: sparse-novelty-anchor-peak
round0.rationale: Implements the assigned sparse-novelty+peak angle: a positive novelty term built from (1 - anchor_max_overlap) — distance from the single closest prior-art neighbor, a true inverse construct per FEATURES.md — blended 60/40 with contrib_peak ("does it have >=1 strong contribution"), plus a light delta_max report-genre penalty (kept small, 0.05, since delta is a corpus confound-proxy not a causal signal — pushing it higher chases train_rho by penalizing genuine findings like aki26 that legitimately report a big verified delta). Two key fixes over the naive version of the angle: (1) multiplied the novelty term by `anchor` (resolution-success/provenance quality: 1.0=fully resolved, 0.2=compiler-estimated) so that papers with an *unresolved* anchor (overlap=None -> coerced to 0 -> falsely max novelty) don't get spuriously rewarded as maximally novel — this is a confidence weight on data provenance, not a quality score, so it doesn't reintroduce anchor's own negative correlation as a quality signal. (2) used anchor_max_overlap (closest single predecessor) instead of anchor_mean_overlap, because a diluted mean can hide one near-duplicate predecessor and mean-overlap let an incremental ML-method paper (afi25, expert 10) score as novel despite a 0.62-overlap near-twin in its resolved neighborhood.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: anchor_resolution_confidence
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: An explicit 0-1 confidence/completeness flag on sota_anchor resolution, separate from the 'anchor' success-reward field, so formulas can distinguish 'genuinely sparse prior art' from 'anchor resolution simply failed/is incomplete' without overloading the anchor field's dual meaning.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Papers like gie25 have anchor_n=4 (neighbors were found) but both anchor_mean_overlap and anchor_max_overlap are null -- the overlap computation itself didn't complete. Under every pure-arithmetic None-guarded formula this null gets treated as overlap=0 (i.e. maximal novelty), which is the opposite of the truth. The current 'anchor' field only partially mitigates this (it gives partial credit, e.g. 0.6, rather than zeroing the term out), so an unresolved-but-partially-scored anchor still leaks unjustified novelty credit into any (1-overlap)-based term.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.primary (normalized, schema-complete)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A consistently-populated genre classification (e.g. primary_research / review / surveillance_report / clinical_trial_readout / methods_paper) across all papers, not just the subset that currently has genre.primary + novelty_prior populated.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The real reason delta_max/n_deltas correlate negatively with expert score is genre (surveillance/burden reports pile up quantified deltas), not delta magnitude itself. Once genre is a clean, fully-schematized field, a metric could condition the delta penalty on genre=='surveillance_report' directly, instead of laundering the genre signal through a delta-count proxy that also penalizes genuine discovery papers (aki26) that happen to report a large verified number.

round0.name: capped_report_tell_v13
round0.rationale: Starts from the given baseline (0.6*contrib_wmean + 0.6*contrib_peak - 0.15*delta_max - 0.04*n_deltas, train 0.63) and makes exactly the two changes assigned: (1) cap the n_deltas report-tell at min(n_deltas, 4) so the penalty saturates instead of growing without bound — a paper with 11 quantified deltas (e.g. ali25, ant26) is charged the same -0.16 as one with 4, so thorough empirical reporting isn't punished proportionally to its thoroughness once past a reasonable threshold; (2) add a genuine positive novelty term 0.2*(1-anchor_mean_overlap), which is the true-inverse-construct case per the causal-signal guidance (novelty IS distance from prior art, so this recovers real signal rather than laundering a confound). delta_max is kept as the direct genre-signature term (report tells still exist and should count, just not unboundedly via the count). Grid search on train: cap swept 1-8 and weight 0.03-0.12 showed a clear ridge at cap=4, weight=0.04 (0.708), essentially flat against cap=5 (0.707) — a broad, non-fragile optimum, not a knife-edge fit. Notably, on this particular corpus the uncapped version scores almost identically (0.710) because none of the high-expert-score papers in this split happen to carry heavy delta counts — so the cap is a free robustness insurance policy here (near-zero cost on train) rather than a train-set win, and its real payoff is protecting against a future/held-out corpus where a genuine deep empirical breakthrough legitimately reports many verified deltas (the aki26 misfire pattern flagged in the brief: expert 70, delta_max 1.0). Kept contrib weights at the baseline's own 0.6/0.6 rather than re-tuning them (grid-checked 0.5-0.7 range and the baseline values were already at or near the local max), to avoid overfitting extra degrees of freedom to 40 training points. 5 terms, no genre field, no dead follow-on features (uptake_per_year, cd_index excluded per instructions).

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized field: genre.primary (enum: primary_research | surveillance_report | review_survey | guideline | pipeline_update) with confidence, consistently emitted for every paper
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, LLM-assigned genre label distinguishing surveillance/burden reports and pipeline/guideline updates from primary empirical research, independent of how many deltas or contributions the paper happens to report.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: The current delta features (n_deltas, delta_max) are being used as an indirect proxy for 'is this a report' because that is the only signal available, but the brief itself flags this as a confound: papers that are genuinely deep empirical breakthroughs (e.g. aki26, expert 70) also carry many verified deltas and get misfired against. A clean genre field would let a metric condition on genre directly (e.g. only dampen delta-heavy scoring for surveillance_report genre) instead of penalizing delta-richness universally, removing the tension between rewarding thoroughness and penalizing report-genre entirely. Right now genre.yaml exists but is inconsistently schematized (some papers have genre.primary + novelty_prior, most only paper_type), so it cannot yet be used safely in a formula.

round0.rationale:
Three roughly equal-weight signals, each targeting a distinct, causally-motivated construct rather than a sign-flipped correlational proxy:

1. DEPTH: contrib_wmean (confidence-weighted mean novelty-type of the paper's own contributions). Chosen over the composite `contribution` or `contrib_peak` alone because wmean specifically cannot be inflated by padding shallow contributions (breadth-immune), giving the strongest single-feature signal (rho +0.50) among the depth family.

2. NOVELTY-DENSITY: anchor * (1 - anchor_mean_overlap). anchor_mean_overlap is the TRUE INVERSE CONSTRUCT for novelty (dense prior-art overlap = incremental, sparse = novel), so (1 - overlap) recovers the real latent variable rather than laundering a proxy. But I found a real gaming hole: 2 of 66 papers (gie25, yu25) have anchor_mean_overlap unresolved/None, which the harness silently coerces to 0 before my formula ever sees it -- turning "we don't know the overlap" into "maximal novelty," and gie25 (expert score 7.7, a low-significance epidemiology piece) rocketed to the #1 slot on train as a result. I gate the novelty term by the `anchor` feature (which encodes resolution confidence/success, 1.0 = fully resolved neighborhood, lower = weaker resolution) so an unresolved anchor can no longer buy free novelty credit. This is a legitimate use of `anchor` as a confidence multiplier, not as a direct quality proxy (its own raw correlation is negative and I'm not claiming resolution-success itself signals breakthrough-ness). This single fix moved train rho from 0.60 to 0.635.

3. REPORT-TELL: -0.10 * n_deltas. This is the one place I deliberately deviated from a maximally-generalizing choice, because the assigned angle calls for a report-tell term, and I wanted the least confound-laundered version available. I used n_deltas (raw COUNT of comparative claims) rather than delta_max (size of the single largest verified delta) specifically because delta_max punishes a paper for having one big, real, verified effect -- exactly the aki26 failure mode called out in the brief (expert 70, delta_max 1.0). n_deltas instead fires on breadth of comparison-making (many baseline comparisons = survey/burden-report structure), and empirically aki26 has only 3 deltas so it survives this term almost unpenalized while genuine multi-comparison reports (ant26, 11 deltas; ahm26b, 6; aku25b, 6) get pushed down. I kept the weight modest (0.10, chosen by scanning 0.02-0.30 and picking near the low end of the plateau rather than the rho-maximizing 0.15) so it acts as a tie-breaker among similar-depth/novelty papers rather than dominating the ranking -- I did not chase the extra ~0.01-0.03 rho available at higher weights because that requires leaning harder on exactly the confound the brief warns is spurious.

I explicitly avoided delta, delta_max, frac_quant_verified, and the dead follow-on features (uptake_per_year, cd_index) per the brief's guidance, and did not add n_contribs/n_puffery/contrib_peak since they added no rho and would violate the 3-term "each ~equal weight" angle.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml normalized to a single required field: genre.primary in a closed taxonomy (e.g. {original_research, surveillance_report, systematic_review, clinical_trial_readout, methods_paper}), populated for every paper (not just some)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A direct, non-gameable signal for 'is this a report/survey vs a discovery paper' -- the thing n_deltas is currently used as a crude, confound-prone stand-in for.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Right now the only report-tell signals available (n_deltas, delta_max, delta) are correlational proxies that happen to fire on surveillance/burden-report genres in THIS corpus but would misfire on a corpus where genuine breakthroughs also report many verified deltas (e.g. a multi-endpoint clinical trial with a real discovery). A clean genre field would let a metric condition report-tell penalties on genre.primary == surveillance_report directly, decoupling 'this paper made many quantified comparisons' (fine, even good, for a trial or discovery paper) from 'this paper's PURPOSE is to summarize/re-estimate known quantities' (the actual thing that should discount breakthrough-ness).

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: anchor_resolution_confidence as an explicit separate field (distinct from anchor's current overloaded 'resolution success' scoring)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean 0-1 confidence score for whether anchor_mean_overlap is trustworthy, decoupled from any implied quality signal.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: I had to repurpose `anchor` (whose own documented meaning conflates resolution success with an implicit quality reward, and whose raw correlation with expert score is negative) as a confidence gate on the novelty term, because there is currently no field that says 'trust this overlap number' without also smuggling in the buggy resolution-success scoring. A dedicated confidence field would let novelty-density gating be principled rather than borrowed from a feature the brief itself calls out as buggy.

round0.name: balanced-depth-novelty-reporttell
harmonic_depth_novelty
round0.rationale:
Harmonic mean of a depth term and a novelty term, per the assigned angle: reward balance, punish imbalance, on the theory that a real breakthrough needs BOTH a genuinely deep contribution AND real distance from prior art — neither alone is sufficient, and the harmonic mean (unlike an arithmetic blend) crashes toward zero if either term is weak, so a paper cannot buy a high score with depth alone while sitting next to a near-duplicate predecessor.

Depth term: contrib_wmean — confidence-weighted mean novelty weight across a paper's contributions. Chosen over contrib_peak/contribution because FEATURES.md flags it as depth-not-breadth and explicitly "cannot be inflated by padding," which matters more once it's yoked to a term (novelty) that also can't be gamed by adding filler.

Novelty term: (1 - anchor_max_overlap) — the causal inverse of the single closest prior-work neighbor's overlap (a TRUE INVERSE CONSTRUCT, same family as anchor_mean_overlap, which the brief explicitly sanctions flipping). I tested anchor_max_overlap against anchor_mean_overlap and their average as the novelty base: max_overlap alone gave train rho 0.705 vs 0.506 for mean_overlap and 0.614 for their average. The likely reason: mean_overlap gets diluted for papers with many resolved neighbors (large anchor_n) even when one neighbor is a near-duplicate, whereas max_overlap asks the sharper, more decisive question "is there already basically this paper" — exactly the near-duplicate-predecessor signal FEATURES.md attributes to anchor_max_overlap.

This directly targets the two known failure modes: (1) it gives the metric its first real positive novelty signal instead of inferring novelty indirectly from delta structure; (2) it punishes deep-but-derivative papers (verified in the ranked output: pure surveillance/report papers like the AD facts-and-figures reports and burden reports, which have low contrib_wmean AND high anchor overlap, fall to the very bottom; aki26 — a genuine discovery the delta-penalty baseline misfires on because it legitimately reports big deltas — ranks #1, matching the expert panel's near-top ranking of 68.3).

I deliberately avoided all delta/n_deltas/n_puffery/n_contribs terms per the CONFOUND-PROXY guidance: they are corpus-specific report-tell proxies, not causal breakthrough signals, and adding them (tested: -0.05*delta_max, -0.05*n_puffery, +0.02*n_contribs) did not improve train rho beyond noise (0.685-0.707) while adding fragility. Two terms, both causal (contribution depth from the paper's own claims; distance from the nearest real predecessor), kept for interpretability and generalization over the 8-term temptation.

Residual known miscalibration: gie25 (geographical inequalities, expert 7.7) and afi25 (vision/CNN transformer review-adjacent ML paper, expert 10.0) still over-rank because they combine moderate contrib_wmean with genuinely low anchor overlap — the metric can't distinguish "sparse prior art because genuinely first-in-field" from "sparse prior art because it's a niche subtopic the resolver under-covered." That gap is a resolver-coverage confound within the PRIOR-ART provenance layer itself, not something fixable by reweighting existing features.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: anchor_neighbors[].contribution_id (a per-contribution anchor link, not just a paper-level anchor)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: Which SPECIFIC contribution in contributions.yaml each sota_anchor neighbor's overlap applies to, so depth and novelty can be paired at the same granularity (this specific strong claim vs its specific nearest predecessor) instead of paper-aggregate contrib_wmean vs paper-aggregate anchor_max_overlap.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Currently the harmonic pairing mixes granularities: contrib_wmean averages across all contributions, anchor_max_overlap is the closest neighbor to the paper as a whole (which may be near-duplicating a DIFFERENT, weaker contribution than the one driving contrib_wmean up). A contribution-level anchor link would let the metric ask 'is THIS deep claim actually novel' rather than 'is the paper deep AND is the paper near something' as two separately-aggregated facts that can talk past each other — this is likely the mechanism behind the gie25/afi25 over-ranking miscalibration noted above.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: resolver_coverage_confidence (per paper, from sota_anchor.yaml)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A confidence/coverage score for how exhaustively the OpenAlex prior-art search covered the paper's actual subfield (e.g. based on search-result count, field-specificity of query terms used).
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Low anchor_max_overlap is currently ambiguous between 'genuinely first-in-field' and 'resolver didn't find the right neighbors because the subfield/venue is under-indexed' (explicitly flagged as a risk in FEATURES.md for anchor_n). Without this, the novelty term in any anchor-based metric can be fooled by search misses in the same direction real novelty pushes it, which is invisible from the anchor fields alone.

round0.name: gated_novelty_bonus
round0.rationale:
Assigned angle: make novelty a genuinely nonlinear, max()-gated bonus rather than a linear sign-flip. anchor_mean_overlap is the one feature in this corpus that is a true inverse construct for novelty (FEATURES.md: low overlap = sparse prior art = genuinely novel; PRIOR-ART provenance, not an LLM self-report, not a genre confound like the delta features). Rather than subtracting overlap linearly across its whole range, I gate it with max(0, T - overlap): papers below the empirical bottom-quartile overlap (T=0.28; corpus quartiles: min .21 / Q1 .40 / median .45 / Q3 .55 / max .72) get a real, size-proportional bonus for being near first-in-field; everything at or above that threshold contributes exactly zero from novelty and is judged purely on contribution depth. That matches the assigned spec precisely: "reward strongly when very low, flat otherwise."

Base term is `contribution` (the already-validated 0.6*peak+0.4*wmean composite from contributions.yaml, solo ρ +0.51) since it is the strongest single causal signal in the substrate and my angle is specifically about the novelty term, not re-litigating the contribution composite's internal weighting. I deliberately excluded delta/n_deltas/delta_max (per the causal-vs-confound guidance these are corpus-specific report-genre proxies, not causal breakthrough signal, and they misfire on aki26) and excluded anchor/uptake_per_year/cd_index (near-zero or dead per the provenance notes).

I grid-searched T in [0.15,0.75] and W in [0.1,0.8], crossed with several contrib_peak/contrib_wmean splits. Two findings: (1) a full-range (near-linear) novelty term, achieved by pushing T toward the corpus max (~0.72), converges to a marginally higher train_rho (~0.573) but that is exactly the opposite of my assigned angle -- it stops being a threshold/flat-otherwise bonus and becomes a disguised linear sign-flip of overlap, which is a weaker and less-generalizing move than a true nonlinear gate. (2) the genuinely nonlinear, low-T gated version plateaus around train_rho 0.55, a real but modest lift over the contribution-only baseline (0.546); most of the corpus's ranking power still comes from contribution depth, and the novelty bonus mainly reorders the handful of near-first-in-field papers (aki26, afi25, han26 all receive a non-zero bonus) without disturbing the rest. I kept the design faithful to the assigned nonlinear spec rather than chasing the higher but angle-violating linear number.

Known residual failure mode NOT fixable by this angle: contrib_wmean can still be inflated by the LLM's own reading of a paper's stated novelty on derivative-but-well-pitched ML/diagnostic papers (e.g. sim26 multi-modal-GNN, expert 26.7, contrib_wmean alone 0.76) -- a contribution-typing bias, not something anchor_mean_overlap can correct, since such papers often carry mid-range (not low) overlap, so my gate correctly assigns them zero novelty bonus; the overscoring lives entirely in the base contribution term and is out of scope for a pure novelty-angle metric.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: genre.yaml (normalized: genre.primary enum + genre.confidence, applied consistently to all papers)
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A clean, consistently-schematized paper-genre tag (e.g. primary_research / surveillance_report / narrative_review / trial_readout / methods_paper) instead of the current inconsistent genre.yaml (only some papers have genre.primary+novelty_prior; most only have paper_type).
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Designers are tempted to launder a genre signal through delta/n_deltas because reports/re-estimates happen to pile up quantified deltas in this corpus. That is a confound proxy that misfires on genuine discoveries that also report big numbers (already misfires on aki26) and would break on a corpus where breakthroughs also carry deltas. A first-class, consistently-populated genre field would let a metric condition on or exclude surveillance/review genres directly and honestly, instead of inferring genre indirectly via a report-count proxy.

proposed_extensions.sidecar_or_field: contribution_external_check: per-contribution cross-check of contributions.yaml typing against sota_anchor.yaml overlap evidence
proposed_extensions.what_it_would_capture: A per-claim flag or discount indicating whether a paper's self-claimed novelty type (new_method, new_paradigm, etc.) is corroborated or contradicted by how close the nearest resolved prior-art neighbor actually is to that specific claim.
proposed_extensions.why_needed: Reconciling contribution typing and anchor overlap only at the claim level (rather than as two independent whole-paper scalars) would let a metric discount specific overclaimed contributions instead of averaging depth across all claims, fixing the deep-but-derivative overscoring failure mode that a whole-paper novelty term structurally cannot reach.

critiques.name: novelty_gated_contribution
critiques.verdict: STRONG: the multiplicative novelty gate (0.4-1.0 range on the whole contribution signal) is interpretable and encodes the causal 'crowded neighborhood discounts stated depth' story cleanly. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: still carries two report-suppression proxies (delta_max + n_deltas) making it a 5-term hybrid, and it uses anchor_mean_overlap, whose own single-feature rho is weaker than anchor_max_overlap that the top scorers used; the 0.4 floor and coefficients are hand-set. IMPROVE: swap anchor_mean_overlap -> anchor_max_overlap and drop the two delta terms to test whether the pure gate retains rho, isolating causal signal from confound.

critiques.name: depth_gated_novelty
critiques.verdict: STRONG: 2nd-highest rho (0.741) and it does something no one else did well - repurposes the known-buggy `anchor` resolution field as a confidence MULTIPLIER on the novelty term, directly neutralizing the unresolved-anchor false-novelty trap (gie25) that nearly every other top formula suffers; single minimal n_deltas penalty. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: `anchor` conflates resolution-success with the density-reward bug, so the gate's semantics are muddy; still leans on the n_deltas genre confound; 5 features total. IMPROVE: replace `anchor` with an explicit resolution-confidence proxy (or a None-vs-zero flag) and report sensitivity to fully dropping n_deltas.

critiques.name: report-suppressor-interaction
critiques.verdict: STRONG: genuinely clever insight that a large delta_max is only a report-tell when co-located with dense prior art, so conditioning the penalty on delta_max*anchor_mean_overlap beats unconditional subtraction; stable coefficient sweep and empirically separates report-genre from real findings. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: it is fundamentally report-SUPPRESSION with no standalone positive novelty term, so it under-serves the 'positive novelty signal' criterion; the interaction rides this corpus's genre<->(delta,overlap) correlation and may not transfer. IMPROVE: add an independent (1-anchor_max_overlap) positive term and check whether the interaction still earns its weight.

critiques.name: multiplicative-depth-x-novelty-max-overlap
critiques.verdict: STRONG: cleanest contender - 3 terms, zero confound proxies, pure depth x positive-novelty product that structurally crushes deep-but-derivative papers; the neutral 0.5 guard for missing overlap is a thoughtful, defensible default. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: shares the anchor_max_overlap=None ambiguity (the 0.5 guard mitigates but also caps genuine first-in-field novelty at 0.5), and the afi25-type resolver-coverage misfire (low overlap = under-indexed subfield, not novel) is unaddressed. IMPROVE: add a resolution-confidence weight so genuinely-sparse prior art and unresolved anchors are no longer collapsed.

critiques.name: peak_dominant_novelty
critiques.verdict: STRONG: clean additive form with no confounds, a sensible `or 1` guard (unresolved anchor -> neutral, not maximal novelty), and honest disclosure of the residual genre misfires it cannot fix. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: nearly redundant with the multiplicative entry but additive, so it loses the 'must be BOTH deep and novel' property that makes the product form principled; the `or 1` guard is a blunt fix. IMPROVE: test the multiplicative or min() combination of the same three features to enforce jointness and likely lift rho past 0.702.

critiques.name: anti_padding_depth
critiques.verdict: STRONG: honest, clean anti-padding control; correctly discovered that n_contribs is NOT a pure confound (only a tiny -0.01 nudge helps) and refused to chase delta-based rho. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: n_puffery is identically zero across the train split (a genuinely dead term contributing nothing), and with no novelty signal the rho (0.569) is not competitive. IMPROVE: add the (1-anchor_max_overlap) novelty term it rejected, but combined MULTIPLICATIVELY rather than additively - it dismissed it after only testing the additive form.

critiques.name: novelty_depth_interaction
critiques.verdict: STRONG: additive-plus-interaction structure avoids zeroing out real breakthroughs whose anchor is densely/poorly resolved, and it honestly refused delta terms despite a ~+0.1 rho temptation. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: low rho (0.574); frac_quant_verified is effectively useless (capped at 0.03, admits it hurts monotonically) and inflates the term count to 5 for no gain. IMPROVE: drop frac_quant_verified entirely and swap anchor_mean_overlap -> anchor_max_overlap in the interaction, which the higher-scoring entries showed is the sharper novelty signal.

critiques.name: min_gate_depth_novelty
critiques.verdict: STRONG: the min(depth, novelty) gate is a strong, interpretable 'must be both' construct on only 2 features, with an honest same-feature tiebreaker. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: low rho (0.559) driven by heavy ties at the novelty floor because many papers cluster at anchor_mean_overlap~=0.65; uses mean_overlap, the weaker of the two overlap features. IMPROVE: switch the novelty leg to (1-anchor_max_overlap) to break the floor ties and sharpen the near-duplicate signal - likely a substantial rho gain for free.

critiques.name: ratio_depth_over_report_tells
critiques.verdict: STRONG: density-normalized saturating penalty depth/(1+k*n_deltas) is a better-shaped report correction than linear subtraction, and by avoiding delta_max it spares genuine discoveries like aki26. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: no positive novelty term - it remains a report-tell approach; the damping constant k=0.6 is fitted, and rho (0.667) trails the novelty-based family. IMPROVE: add a positive (1-anchor_max_overlap) term outside the ratio, or replace the n_deltas denominator with a genre field once available, to stop normalizing by a genre proxy.

critiques.name: anchor-neighborhood-confidence-gated-novelty
critiques.verdict: STRONG: highest rho (0.753) with only 2 terms, and the anchor_n confidence gate is a genuinely original, defensible reliability weight - it correctly REJECTS the naive 'few neighbors = novel' story and instead uses anchor_n to say 'trust the low-overlap reading only if a real neighborhood was resolved.' WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: the min(anchor_n,9)/9 saturation cut at 9 is a fitted hyperparameter; anchor_n itself correlates positively (a coverage artifact) so the gate risks smuggling that in; still hits the gie25 None-trap (disclosed). IMPROVE: cross-validate the saturation point on held-out data and add a real None-vs-zero-overlap distinction rather than relying on the arithmetic None-guard.

critiques.name: contribution_quality_control
critiques.verdict: STRONG: valuable pure-contribution control that cleanly establishes the self-report ceiling (~0.55) and is maximally interpretable - a useful floor for the tournament. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: by design it has no novelty or genre correction so it is a baseline, not a contender, and it double-counts contrib_wmean (once inside the `contribution` composite, once standalone). IMPROVE: as a competitor it would need the orthogonal (1-anchor_max_overlap) novelty term; as a control it is already doing its job and should be retained as the reference point.

critiques.name: sparse-novelty-anchor-peak
critiques.verdict: STRONG: a positive (1-anchor_max_overlap) novelty term gated by `anchor` resolution-confidence to block unresolved-anchor false novelty, plus a deliberately small delta_max weight. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: it mixes the buggy `anchor` field with a residual delta_max confound, and it overlaps heavily with depth_gated_novelty while scoring lower (0.691 vs 0.741). IMPROVE: drop the delta_max term and benchmark directly against depth_gated_novelty's cleaner anchor-gated construction to see if the delta is doing anything but adding confound risk.

critiques.name: capped_report_tell_v13
critiques.verdict: STRONG: capping n_deltas at 4 is sensible robustness insurance against punishing thorough empirical papers, and it adds a genuine positive novelty term to the baseline. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: 5 terms and still baseline-report-suppression-dominant; the author admits the cap is free-on-train (0.708 vs 0.710 uncapped), meaning nearly all the rho comes from the delta confound plus only a 0.2-weight novelty term. IMPROVE: raise the novelty weight and ablate delta_max to quantify how much of the 0.708 is confound-driven versus causal.

critiques.name: balanced-depth-novelty-reporttell
critiques.verdict: STRONG: three roughly-equal causal constructs with an anchor-confidence gate that fixes the None-trap (0.60 -> 0.635), and it deliberately uses n_deltas (count) rather than delta_max (magnitude) to spare aki26. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: moderate rho (0.635); n_deltas is still a genre proxy, and it uses the weaker mean_overlap. IMPROVE: switch to anchor_max_overlap and either drop or genre-condition the n_deltas term - the two changes the higher scorers made.

critiques.name: harmonic_depth_novelty
critiques.verdict: STRONG: the harmonic mean is a genuinely distinct aggregation that enforces balance (score crashes if EITHER depth or novelty is weak) on just 2 confound-free features with a real positive novelty signal - high diversity value and 0.705 rho. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: uses the `or 0` guard, which is the WORST choice for the anchor_max_overlap=None cases (treats missing as maximal novelty), so gie25/afi25 resolver-coverage misfires are baked in and disclosed. IMPROVE: change the guard to neutral (`or 0.5` or `or 1`) and add a resolution-confidence factor - this should both raise rho and remove the known false positives.

critiques.name: gated_novelty_bonus
critiques.verdict: STRONG: a faithful, interpretable nonlinear threshold gate (reward only bottom-quartile overlap, flat otherwise) that is honestly generalization-minded and grid-searched transparently. WEAK/OVERFIT-RISK: low rho (0.55); the threshold T=0.28 is fitted to THIS corpus's overlap quartiles (direct overfit risk), and the bonus only reorders a handful of near-first-in-field papers.
